1: A theme: "Have mercy on me, O God, according to your steadfast love; according to your abundant compassion wipe out my transgressions." The superscription grounds this psalm in David's confession after Nathan's confrontation (2 Samuel 12:1-14) — the paradigmatic penitential prayer. The petition for mercy (hānan) and covenant love (hesed) is the fundamental prayer structure the NT assumes. Luke 18:13 (the tax collector: "God, have mercy on me, a sinner") is built on the same cry. Christ is the one through whom the mercy of Psalms 51:1 is channeled: Hebrews 4:16 ("receive mercy and find grace to help us in our time of need") grounds this in our access to the throne of grace through the great high priest.
2: A theme: "Scrub away my guilt completely, and purify me from my sin." The plea for comprehensive moral cleansing uses two washing metaphors: kābas (the vigorous scrubbing of soiled garments) and ṭāhēr (ritual purification). 1 John 1:7: "the blood of Jesus, his Son, purifies us from all sin" — the purification that Levitical ritual could only symbolize, Christ accomplishes definitively. Hebrews 10:22: "let us draw near to God with a sincere heart and with the full assurance that faith brings, having our hearts sprinkled to cleanse us from a guilty conscience." The scrubbing and purifying of Psalms 51:2 is what Christ's blood accomplishes for the conscience.
3: A theme: "For I know my rebellions, and my sin is before me at every moment." The acknowledged omnipresence of sin — sin that cannot be ignored or forgotten — is the spiritual condition that precedes and enables genuine repentance. Romans 7:24 (Paul: "What a wretched man I am! Who will rescue me from this body that is subject to death?") expresses the same relentless awareness of moral failure. The answer in Romans 7:25 is "Jesus Christ our Lord." The sin that is "before me at every moment" in Psalms 51:3 drives the penitent to the one who alone can remove it from God's sight permanently (Hebrews 10:17: "their sins and lawless acts I will remember no more").
4: A direct citation: "Against you — you alone — have I sinned and done what is evil in your sight, so that you are proved right when you speak and blameless when you judge." Paul quotes the last clause in Romans 3:4 as proof that God is righteous in his justifying judgment: "let God be true, and every human being a liar. As it is written: 'So that you may be proved right when you speak and prevail when you judge.'" The theological move is striking: David's confession of sin (which vindicates God's judgment against him) becomes Paul's proof that God's justifying verdict in Christ is righteous. The God proved right in judging David is proved right in justifying the sinner through Christ's atoning death.
5: A theme: "Surely I was brought forth into guilt, and in sin my mother conceived me." The ontological depth of sin — not merely acts but the condition of existence from conception — is the anthropological claim behind Paul's doctrine of original sin (Romans 5:12-14). This is not a statement about the sinfulness of sexual union (the Fathers sometimes misread it thus) but about the solidarity of the human race in Adam's fallen condition. The need for new birth (John 3:3-8), new creation (2 Corinthians 5:17), and the second Adam (1 Corinthians 15:45-49) are all responses to the condition that Psalms 51:5 names: sin at the level of origin, not merely behavior.
6: A revelation of God: "You desire truth deep inside a person; in the hidden place you are teaching me wisdom." The divine desire for inner truth — the integrity of tûmāh (truth/sincerity) in the hidden depths (ṭûhôt) of the person — is what Christ identifies as the heart of the commandment (Matthew 5:8: "blessed are the pure in heart, for they will see God"; Matthew 5:28: inward desire = the act). John 4:23-24 ("God is spirit, and his worshipers must worship in the Spirit and in truth") develops the "inner truth" of v6 into the pneumatological reality that Christ enables. The wisdom taught "in the hidden place" is the wisdom of the Spirit: 1 Corinthians 2:10 ("the Spirit searches all things, even the deep things of God").
7: A shadow: "Cleanse me with hyssop, and I will be clean; wash me, and I will be whiter than snow." Hyssop (ēzôv) is the ritual instrument of purification in the Mosaic law — used to apply blood in the Passover (Exodus 12:22), in the cleansing of skin disease (Leviticus 14:4, 6, 51), and in the red heifer purification (Numbers 19:6, 18). At the crucifixion, hyssop is the instrument used to offer Jesus the sour wine (John 19:29). The hyssop of Psalms 51:7 — the instrument of ritual cleansing — becomes at Golgotha the instrument through which the ultimate cleansing sacrifice is administered. The ritual shadow of purification-by-hyssop is enacted in reverse at the cross.
8: A theme: "Let me hear the sounds of joy and gladness; let the bones you have crushed be glad." The crushed bones as the somatic image of guilt under divine judgment — and the hope for their restoration to joy — is a pattern the resurrection fulfills. Hebrews 12:11: "no discipline seems pleasant at the time, but painful; later on, however, it produces a harvest of righteousness and peace for those who have been trained by it." The bones crushed by divine discipline are healed not by the removal of the discipline but by its completion in Christ's suffering (1 Peter 4:1: "since Christ suffered in his body, arm yourselves with the same attitude").
9: A theme: "Look away from my sins; wipe out all my guilt." The plea for divine non-seeing of sin — the averting of the divine face from the record of transgression — is what atonement accomplishes. Hebrews 10:17 (citing Jeremiah 31:34): "their sins and lawless acts I will remember no more." The mechanism of this divine non-seeing is Christ's sacrifice: "God presented Christ as a sacrifice of atonement... He did this to demonstrate his righteousness, because in his forbearance he had left the sins committed beforehand unpunished" (Romans 3:25). The wiping out of guilt in Psalms 51:9 is the "blotting out" (Colossians 2:14: "having canceled the charge of our legal indebtedness... he took it away, nailing it to the cross").
10: A direct anticipation: "Create a pure heart in me, O God, and renew a steadfast spirit within me." The verb bārā' (create) is used here — the same word used only for divine creative action in the OT (Genesis 1:1, 21, 27; Psalms 2:3, 4; Isaiah 40:26, 28; Psalms 45:12, 18). David is not asking for moral improvement but for a new creation. 2 Corinthians 5:17: "if anyone is in Christ, the new creation has come: the old has gone, the new is here!" John 3:3-8 (born again from above by the Spirit) and Ezekiel 36:26 ("I will give you a new heart and put a new spirit in you") are the Christological and pneumatological fulfillments of this petition. The new creation of v10 is what Christ enables through the Spirit.
11: A direct anticipation: "Do not banish me from your presence; do not take your Holy Spirit from me." The fear of the loss of the Holy Spirit — ruah qodshekha (your Holy Spirit) — reflects the OT's conditional pneumatology: the Spirit could be withdrawn from Saul (1 Samuel 16:14). Christ's promise in John 14:16-17 is the definitive answer to this fear: "I will ask the Father, and he will give you another advocate to help you and be with you forever — the Spirit of truth." The Spirit given through Christ is not taken away; the fear of Psalms 51:11 is met by the permanence of the Spirit's indwelling in the new covenant community.
12: A theme: "Give back to me the joy of your rescue, and sustain me with a generous, willing spirit." The "joy of rescue" — the gladness that belongs to the one who has experienced divine salvation — is what Christ restores to those who come to him. John 15:11: "I have told you this so that my joy may be in you and that your joy may be complete." The "willing spirit" (nādîvāh — generous, noble, freely-giving) that is asked as a sustaining gift is the Spirit that Christ pours out on those who believe (Acts 2:17-18: "I will pour out my Spirit on all people"). The rescue-joy and the willing spirit are both gifts of the new covenant through Christ.
13: A theme: "Then I will show other rebels your ways, and sinners will come back to you." The missional consequence of personal restoration — the forgiven one becoming the instrument of others' repentance — is the pattern of the apostolic testimony. Peter's restoration after his denials (John 21:15-19) leads directly to his Pentecost proclamation (Acts 2:14-41). Paul's pre-Christian violence against the church did not disqualify him; his testimony became a means of others' conversion (1 Timothy 1:15-16). The "showing of rebels God's ways" in Psalms 51:13 is the evangelistic fruit that genuine repentance and restoration produce.
14: A theme: "Rescue me from the guilt of bloodshed, O God — God of my salvation — and my tongue will ring out your righteousness." The plea for rescue from blood-guilt (dāmîm) — the most serious form of moral pollution in the OT (Num 35; 2 Sam 12) — points to the ultimate blood-guilt: the crucifixion itself, in which innocent blood was shed. Acts 5:28 (the Sanhedrin: "you have filled Jerusalem with your teaching and are determined to make us guilty of this man's blood") acknowledges the blood-guilt of the crucifixion. Christ's blood, paradoxically, "speaks a better word than the blood of Abel" (Hebrews 12:24) — not crying out for vengeance but for forgiveness of the very blood-guilt it creates.
15: A theme: "O Lord, open my lips, and my mouth will declare your praise." The request for divinely-opened lips — the acknowledgment that even praise requires divine enabling — is the posture of the NT community at prayer. Acts 2:4: "all of them were filled with the Holy Spirit and began to speak in other tongues as the Spirit enabled them." Ephesians 6:19: "pray also for me, that whenever I speak, words may be given me so that I will fearlessly make known the mystery of the gospel." The lips that God opens declare his praise — and the ultimate praise is the proclamation of Christ crucified and risen, which only the Spirit enables (1 Corinthians 12:3).
16: A direct anticipation: "You do not want sacrifice — otherwise I would bring it; you take no delight in burnt offerings." The prophetic principle that sacrifice is not YHWH's primary desire (cf. 1 Samuel 15:22; Isaiah 1:11-13; Hosea 6:6; Micah 6:6-8) is the OT critique of formalism that the NT fulfills. Hebrews 10:5-9 quotes Psalms 40:6-8 (LXX: "sacrifice and offering you did not desire... then I said, 'Here I am — it is written about me in the scroll — I have come to do your will, my God'") and applies it to Christ: "he sets aside the first [the sacrificial system] to establish the second [his obedience]." The sacrifice God does not want (animal offerings) gives way to the sacrifice he does: Christ's body.
17: A direct anticipation: "What God wants as sacrifice is a broken spirit; a broken and crushed heart, O God, you will not reject." The theology of the broken spirit as the acceptable sacrifice — replacing animal offerings with the moral and spiritual self — is fulfilled in Christ in two ways: (1) Christ himself was "broken" in the passion (Isaiah 53:10: "it pleased the LORD to crush him"), his spirit the ultimate broken offering; and (2) the community he forms is invited to bring broken hearts as worship. Romans 12:1: "offer your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and pleasing to God — this is your true and proper worship." The broken spirit that God will not reject is the heart of NT sacrificial theology.
18: A theme: "In your favor, do good to Zion; build up the walls of Jerusalem." The psalm's closing intercession for the restoration of Jerusalem — the communal vision after the individual's personal restoration — reflects the theology that the individual's righteousness is connected to the community's flourishing. In the NT, the "building up" that matters is the church: Ephesians 4:12 ("for the equipping of his people for works of service, so that the body of Christ may be built up") and 1 Peter 2:5 ("you also, like living stones, are being built into a spiritual house"). Christ builds his church (Matthew 16:18), the new Jerusalem, as the restoration of what Israel's sin had broken.
19: A theme: "Then you will be pleased with right sacrifices, with burnt offerings and whole offerings; then bulls will go up on your altar." The closing verse restores sacrificial practice to its proper place — not replaced by repentance but re-grounded in it. This is not a contradiction of v16-17 but its fulfillment: sacrifice offered from a broken and contrite heart is the sacrifice God accepts. Hebrews 9:14: "how much more will the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself unblemished to God, cleanse our consciences from acts that lead to death, so that we may serve the living God!" The "right sacrifice" of Psalms 51:19 is Christ's self-offering from a perfect, unbroken will — the one sacrifice that makes all subsequent offerings of praise and service acceptable.
